fire_planner.guide.step1_body: Enter your current age, target FIRE age, and monthly spend in retirement. Use today's money — inflation is handled automatically. Saving will also create a "FIRE" goal in your portfolio, visible on the main Dashboard.
fire_planner.guide.step2_body: Enter how much you invest each month and your expected annual portfolio return (7% is a common long-term figure for a diversified equity portfolio). If you enter your annual salary the planner shows your savings rate and can grow contributions with raises.
fire_planner.guide.step2_title: Set your monthly contribution and expected return
fire_planner.guide.step3_body: Each stream has a monthly amount (in today's money) and a payout start age. Streams active from your FIRE age reduce how much the portfolio must cover. Deferred streams (e.g. state pension at 67) are modelled as separate phases that kick in later.
fire_planner.guide.step4_body: Enable "Has accumulation fund" on the stream. Enter the current fund balance, monthly contribution, and the fund's expected annual return. Link it to the matching Wealthfolio account to pull the live balance with one click. The planner accumulates the fund until FIRE, then lets it grow contribution-free until payout age.
fire_planner.guide.step4_title_default: Pension accumulation funds (provident funds, employer schemes)
fire_planner.guide.step5_body: The Dashboard shows your FIRE target, progress, budget breakdown by source, and a year-by-year table. Simulations runs Monte Carlo (100,000 paths), scenario analysis, sensitivity heatmaps, and sequence-of-returns crash tests.
fire_planner.guide.step5_title: Read the Dashboard, then explore Simulations
fire_planner.guide.term_coast_body: If you stopped contributing today, this is the minimum portfolio needed to grow to your FIRE target by your target age on investment returns alone. Once you pass Coast FIRE, every additional contribution accelerates retirement rather than enabling it.
fire_planner.guide.term_fire_target_net_: The portfolio you need to accumulate, after subtracting income available from day one of FIRE. A €1,200/mo stream that starts at your FIRE age reduces your target by €1,200 × 12 / SWR. Deferred streams (e.g. state pension at 67) do NOT reduce this number — your portfolio must bridge the gap on its own until those streams start.
fire_planner.guide.term_gross_net_body: The gross target (monthly expenses × 12 / SWR) ignores income streams. The net target subtracts day-one income, giving the actual amount your portfolio needs to accumulate. The Dashboard shows the net target as the primary figure. The FIRE goal synced to your portfolio uses the gross target — the number you "see" as your savings milestone.
fire_planner.guide.term_horizon_body: How long the portfolio must last. The FIRE target and all simulations run to this age. Set it to 90–95 to stress-test longevity. The Sequence of Returns and Monte Carlo success rate are both measured at this horizon.
fire_planner.guide.term_income_projectio: Stacked area chart of each income stream over time versus your inflation-adjusted expense line. The gap between the stack and the line is the portfolio withdrawal needed each year. The coverage table shows what fraction of expenses are self-funded at each key age.
fire_planner.guide.term_inflation_body: All projections grow expenses and inflation-linked income at this rate year over year. The default 2% matches the ECB target.
fire_planner.guide.term_inflation_it_suf: Use 2.5–3% for a more conservative Italian CPI assumption.
fire_planner.guide.term_it_fondo_body: Add it as an income stream and enable "Has accumulation fund". Enter the current fund balance (or link the Wealthfolio account and click Sync), the monthly TFR contribution, and the fund's net annual return (check your fund's factsheet — 3–5% after fees is common). Set the payout start age to when you plan to draw it (typically 65–67). The planner accumulates contributions until FIRE, then lets the fund grow without new contributions until payout age. The linked account is automatically included in your FIRE goal allocations.
fire_planner.guide.term_it_inps_body: Add it as a plain income stream (no accumulation fund). Enter your estimated monthly net pension in <0>today's euros</0> (real value) and set the payout start age (typically 67 for the contributivo system). Enable "Inflation-adjusted" since INPS is indexed to the cost of living. Use the INPS simulator (inps.it) to estimate your amount — the simulator gives a future nominal value, so convert it to today's euros by dividing by (1 + inflation)^years. Example: simulator says €1,500 at age 67 in 35 years at 2% inflation → enter €1,500 / 1.02³⁵ ≈ €750.
fire_planner.guide.term_it_portfolio_bod: This is your main portfolio — the value Wealthfolio tracks. Set your expected return and monthly contribution here. It is the primary accumulation engine of your FIRE plan.
fire_planner.guide.term_it_tfr_body: TFR is typically paid into the fondo pensione — model it as the monthly contribution on that stream. Rough estimate: gross monthly salary × 6.91%. If you keep TFR in azienda instead, it grows at 1.5% + 75% of the ISTAT index — use a lower accumulationReturn (≈ 2–3%) with no monthly contribution.
fire_planner.guide.term_it_tfr_title: TFR (Trattamento di Fine Rapporto — severance accrual)
fire_planner.guide.term_monte_carlo_body: Runs your plan 100,000 times using a two-regime fat-tailed return distribution: 85% of years draw from a normal distribution centred on your expected return, 15% are stress years with heavier downside. Inflation is stochastic per year. The fan chart shows percentile bands P10–P90. Success rate = % of simulations where the portfolio survives to your planning horizon. Aim for ≥ 90%.
fire_planner.guide.term_monthly_budget_b: Your total monthly spend broken down by funding source. Income streams active from FIRE age appear as coloured segments; the remainder is what the portfolio must cover. Each deferred stream shows how the mix shifts at the age it starts.
fire_planner.guide.term_return_vol_body: Expected return is the average annual growth of your portfolio. For a diversified global equity portfolio, 6–8% (nominal) is a common long-term assumption. Volatility (std dev) is used only in Monte Carlo — higher values produce a wider fan of outcomes. 12% is typical for a mixed equity/bond portfolio.
fire_planner.guide.term_scenario_body: Three return assumptions: pessimistic (−2%), base case, and optimistic (+1.5%). Shows how sensitive your FIRE date is to return differences. Income streams are fully reflected — the lines diverge in the FIRE phase where withdrawals differ.
fire_planner.guide.term_sensitivity_body: Two heatmaps: FIRE age across contribution levels × return rates, and FIRE age across SWR × return rates. Your current settings are highlighted in blue. Use this to identify which lever — saving more, earning more, or spending less — matters most.
fire_planner.guide.term_sorr_body: Five crash scenarios starting from your FIRE date. A crash in year 1 is far more dangerous than the same crash in year 10 because early withdrawals lock in losses. Substantial income streams reduce SORR significantly — a green banner appears when streams cover more than 30% of expenses.
fire_planner.guide.term_strategy_compari: Compares constant-dollar withdrawal (fixed real amount each year) against constant-percentage withdrawal (fixed % of remaining portfolio). Constant-% mathematically never depletes the portfolio but annual spending fluctuates with markets. Run "Compare Strategies" to see which fits your risk tolerance.
fire_planner.guide.term_swr_body: The % of your portfolio you withdraw annually in retirement. The classic 4% rule (Trinity Study) means a €1M portfolio supports €40k/year. This planner defaults to 3.5% — more conservative and appropriate for early retirees with longer horizons. Lower SWR = larger required portfolio = more safety.
fire_planner.guide.term_year_by_year_bod: The accumulation phase shows annual contributions and portfolio growth. The FIRE phase shows annual expenses, income from each stream, and the net withdrawal from the portfolio. The highlighted row marks when FIRE is reached; blue rows mark when a new income stream activates.

performance.error_negative_portfolio_his: Invalid input: The account had negative portfolio values during this period. This may be caused by missing buy activities. Please review your transactions on the Activities page.
performance.metric.annualized_return_inf: Annualized Return shows the geometric average amount of money earned by an investment each year over the selected period, as if the returns were compounded annually.
performance.metric.max_drawdown_info: Maximum Drawdown represents the largest percentage decline from a peak to a subsequent trough in portfolio value during the specified period. It indicates downside risk.
performance.metric.mwr_info: Money-Weighted Return (MWR) measures portfolio performance while accounting for the size and timing of cash flows. It corresponds to the portfolio's internal rate of return (IRR).
performance.metric.twr_info: Time-Weighted Return (TWR) measures the compound growth rate of a portfolio, ignoring the impact of cash flows (deposits/withdrawals). It isolates the performance of the underlying investments.
performance.metric.volatility_info: Volatility measures the dispersion of returns for a given investment. Higher volatility means the price of the investment can change dramatically over a short time period in either direction.
performance.metric.holdings_mode_volatil: Volatility of account value changes based on daily valuations, without cash-flow adjustments for deposits or withdrawals. It reflects how strongly market value fluctuates.
performance.metric.holdings_mode_max_dra: Largest peak-to-trough decline in account value based on daily valuations, without cash-flow adjustments for deposits or withdrawals.

settings.research_links.help.seeking_alp:
Full example: https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/{{symbol}}
Shortcut: paste only https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ (with or without a trailing slash) and the ticker is filled in automatically.
settings.research_links.help.stockanalys:
StockAnalysis uses different paths: US stocks (/stocks/slug/) and US ETFs (/etf/slug/). Outside the US they use /quote/{exchange}/slug/ with a short exchange code — e.g. tsx or tsxv (Canada), etr (Germany/Xetra-style), swx (Switzerland). US listings have no exchange segment.
Placeholders: {{stockanalysisSlug}} (lowercase), {{stockanalysisExchangeSlug}} (empty for US), {{stockanalysisStocksUrl}}, {{stockanalysisEtfUrl}}, {{stockanalysisQuoteUrl}}, {{stockanalysisUrl}} (picks quote vs stocks vs ETF from market).
Shortcut: paste only https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/, https://stockanalysis.com/etf/, or https://stockanalysis.com/ — without {{…}} they expand to {{stockanalysisUrl}} per security.
settings.research_links.help.tips.body: Use “Open in system browser” on a custom row to reuse logins (Seeking Alpha, TradingView, etc.). Built-in providers follow the global “Open links in” setting above.
settings.research_links.help.tradingview:
{{tradingviewSymbol}} is like NASDAQ:AAPL. {{tradingviewSymbolQuery}} is the same value URL-encoded for ?symbol=.
Example: https://www.tradingview.com/chart/?symbol={{tradingviewSymbolQuery}} (you can use de.tradingview.com the same way).
The random segment in /chart/SoMeId/ is an optional saved layout from your TradingView account—you can omit it.
settings.research_links.custom.descripti: Up to three HTTPS templates per row. Turn on “Open in system browser” when the site should use your normal browser session. Details and examples are in Help below.
settings.research_links.custom.open_in_s: Always uses your default browser for this link (e.g. Seeking Alpha or TradingView if you are already signed in there).

settings.market_data.provider_descriptio: Alpha Vantage provides free APIs for real-time and historical data on stocks, forex, and cryptocurrencies.
Börse Frankfurt provides bond and security pricing data for European markets.
Finnhub provides real-time stock, forex, and cryptocurrency data with global coverage. Free tier includes 60 API calls per minute.
MarketData.app provides real-time and historical data for U.S. stocks, options, ETFs, mutual funds, and more.
Provides real-time and historical spot prices for precious metals (gold, silver, platinum, palladium).
OpenFIGI provides a mapping service for financial instrument identifiers (FIGI, ISIN, CUSIP, ticker).
Calculates U.S. Treasury bond prices from yield curve data published by the U.S. Treasury Department.
Yahoo Finance is a leading financial data provider for many markets. It provides historical and real-time stock data.
